text: Hi friends, this is a video of a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy for a patient with ulcerative proliferative growth at the level of the ascending colon just above the cecum.
The surgeon divides the ileocolic artery and vein at their origin, using surgical instruments to carefully separate these vessels during the laparoscopic right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon applies traction to assess the length of the iliacolic vessels, which appear adequate. Upon division, the vessels are seen flush with the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and superior mesenteric vein (SMV).

The surgeon carefully pushes the duodenum and the fascia covering the kidney downward to access the correct anatomical plane, aiming to prevent bleeding and protect vital structures during the laparoscopic right hemicolectomy.
The duodenum is visible in the surgical field, and the surgeon is taking down flimsy attachments to push the duodenum further away from the operative area.
The laparoscopic camera is positioned through the suprapubic port, providing a view from below upwards towards the liver and right shoulder, rather than a perpendicular angle to the surgical area.

After dividing the iliacolic vessels, the surgeon provides a panoramic view of the duodenum and begins further dissection upwards, revealing the pancreas clearly. Three working ports are visible: the main surgeon's port in the parumbilical region slightly to the left, the left-hand port in the right iliac fossa, and another port in the epigastric region towards the left.

The surgeon dissects along the superior mesenteric vessel axis, with the head of the pancreas visible in the field. Using a blunt instrument, the surgeon develops the plane, removing additional flimsy adhesions to the duodenum, which is then pushed completely away from the operating field.
The surgeon dissects the mesocolon on the anterior surface of the pancreas. To the right of the instrument, the blue vein, identified as the right branch of the middle colic vein, is visible.

The surgeon dissects towards the right colic branch, identifying the right colic artery and vein. The artery is isolated, and the vein is visible posteriorly, with careful separation from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon isolates both the right colic artery and vein, ligates them using metal clips, and divides them between the clips. The vein is further isolated to ensure secure application of the clip.
The surgical team continues dissection at the level of the lower border of the pancreas, close to the root of the vessels, adhering to oncological principles for a right hemicolectomy.
The surgeon divides the right colic artery, revealing its lumen clearly. A small flimsy adhesion is also divided. The right colic vein is ligated using a clip and divided between clips.

The surgeon divides a small adhesion on the anterior surface of the pancreas to enter the lesser sac, creating a visible defect. A piece of gauze is placed on the dissected area at the entry point into the lesser curvature.
The surgeon approaches the dissected space from the lateral portion, using the gauze placed earlier as a visual marker to identify the area during the laparoscopic right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon continues the dissection below the pancreas to identify the gonadal vessels and ureter, using minimal traction to enter the correct anatomical planes. The serratus fascia covering the perinephric fat is visible below the dissection area, indicating the correct surgical plane.
The surgeon completes the medial dissection, revealing distinct planes between the perinephric fat and mesocolic fat, with expanded spaces visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon employs a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to carefully navigate the tissue planes, minimizing bleeding and maintaining a clear surgical field.

The surgeon completes the medial dissection, revealing the kidney with its perinephric fat and gyrotrophsia. The ureter and gonadal vessels are visible, covered by fascia that is carefully preserved. Peristalsis is observed in the bowel. A large mass is seen at the level of the ascending colon. The surgeon then proceeds to the lateral side, incising the peritoneum and dividing the lateral attachments of the colon along the line of Toldt.

The patient is positioned with the right lateral side elevated and head tilted up to shift the small bowel towards the left and pelvis, creating space for the appendix. The surgeon uses the appendix for traction to prevent injury to the colon or mesentery. The lateral attachment of the mesocolon is divided along the line of Toldt, revealing a bluish color beneath the peritoneum, indicating prior medial to lateral dissection. The assistant provides medial traction to tension the peritoneum, facilitating entry into the correct plane.

The surgeon uses two instruments to bluntly open the dissected space, aided by carbon dioxide insufflation. The lateral attachment of the colon is divided and the dissection is extended up to the hepatic flexure from the lateral side.
The surgeon identifies and divides a small amount of fat adherent to the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon approaches the hepatic flexure, with the liver visible in the surgical field. The lateral attachment near the hepatic flexure is released, completing the dissection from the medial to lateral side. The peritoneal attachment is divided, allowing entry into the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon carries out dissection in the supracolic compartment, dividing the gastrocolic omentum to enter the lesser sac. The proximal portion of the transverse colon is divided and taken down as part of the right hemicolectomy, addressing the growth at the beginning of the ascending colon.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the hepatic flexure of the transverse colon, ensuring there is no undue tension. The specimen is retrieved through a five-centimeter incision at the umbilicus for extracorporeal anastomosis. The lesser sac is opened, and the peritoneal attachment is further freed.

The surgeon divides the last attachments to completely free the hepatic flexure, ensuring the transverse colon is fully mobilized. The dissection reveals perinephric fat and Gerota's fascia below the dissection level. The transverse colon is now free of attachments, and the surgeon carefully mobilizes the specimen to prevent any strands from pulling it.

The surgeon displays the completely mobilized specimen, free of any attachments. Visible anatomical structures include the kidney with perinephric fat and Gerota's fascia, gallbladder, liver, and the duodenum, which is fully kocherized. The head of the pancreas is seen medial to the duodenum, with the stomach positioned above. A small fibrous strand is divided, and a gas piece used in dissection is visible. A drain is placed subhepatically, and the omentum is pulled down to cover the anastomosis site. The final view shows the hand-sewn anastomosis between the small bowel.
